pattern: Binary search on the value range combined with a count-of-elements-no-greater-than-mid traversal finds the kth smallest in O(n log(max-min)) time.
hints: The answer lies between matrix[0][0] and matrix[n-1][n-1]; binary search on that value range rather than on indices.
For a candidate mid-value, count how many matrix elements are ≤ mid by traversing each row from the right — since rows are sorted, stop advancing left when the element exceeds mid.
Binary search: if count(mid) >= k the answer is ≤ mid so narrow right, otherwise narrow left; the loop converges to the smallest value with at least k elements ≤ it.

edge_inputs.description: duplicate values across rows and columns
all identical elements, any k returns that value

stress.description: large n x n matrix with sorted rows and columns
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 250))
    # build by choosing n*n sorted values and placing them
    vals = sorted(rng.randrange(-10**9, 10**9+1) for _ in range(n*n))
    matrix = [vals[i*n:(i+1)*n] for i in range(n)]
    # rows are sorted; columns: val[i][j] <= val[i+1][j] by construction since
    # vals is sorted and rows are consecutive blocks — not guaranteed column-sorted.
    # Build properly: fill column by column with sorted values so both axes sort.
    import random
    # Simpler: use a grid where matrix[i][j] = base + row_offset*i + col_offset*j
    base = rng.randrange(-1000, 0)
    row_step = rng.randrange(1, 20)
    col_step = rng.randrange(1, 20)
    matrix = [[base + row_step*i + col_step*j for j in range(n)] for i in range(n)]
    k = rng.randrange(1, n*n+1)
    return [matrix, k]
